title: Unlocking Hidden Patterns: How Daily Returns Predict Future Stock Performance
abstract: This Alpha Architect review of a January 2026 academic paper shows that the distribution of a stock's past 21 daily returns contains rich predictive information for next-month performance beyond any single anomaly (MAX, short-term reversal, etc.). Using elastic-net regression estimated annually on inception-to-date US data (1937-2024), the authors construct a Daily Return Information (DRI) signal that combines the chronological sequence and rank-order of daily returns. The corresponding DRIF (Daily Return Information Factor) long-short decile portfolio earns 'economically large premia', subsumes most short-horizon and lottery-style anomalies, holds across subsamples, and remains significant after controlling for the modern factor zoo. Universe: broad US stocks excluding sub-$5 prices and bottom 1% NYSE market cap.
authors: Larry Swedroe (summarising Nusret Cakici, Christian Fieberg, Gabor Neszveda, Robert Bianchi, and Adam Zaremba)
body:
Original paper: 'A Unified Framework for Anomalies based on Daily Returns' by Nusret Cakici (FU), Christian Fieberg (CUAS), Gabor Neszveda (JNU), Robert Bianchi (GU), and Adam Zaremba. January 2026. SSRN: https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=6005614

Alpha Architect blog post: https://alphaarchitect.com/daily-stock-returns/ (published approximately March 25, 2026). Also summarised at: https://larryswedroe.substack.com/p/unlocking-hidden-patterns

The Key Question:
The academic literature is filled with anomalies that use recent daily price movements — short-term reversal (when returns occurred), MAX effect (how extreme returns were), and many others. Each anomaly isolates just one aspect of the same raw material: the sequence of daily returns over the past month. What if we let the data determine the optimal weighting?

Methodology:
- Universe: broad US stocks, January 1937 - December 2024 (87 years), excluding stocks priced under $5 and bottom 1% of NYSE market cap by month-end
- Construct two components from each stock's past 21 daily returns:
  1. Chronological component: time-ordered sequence of returns (captures when returns occurred — linked to short-term price pressure and liquidity effects)
  2. Rank component: magnitude-ordered returns (captures how extreme each daily outcome was — linked to behavioral distortions)
- Combine via elastic-net regression, re-estimated annually on all inception-to-date training data
- This produces the Daily Return Information (DRI) signal
- Long-short DRIF portfolio: each month long (short) the top (bottom) decile of stocks ranked by DRI

Key Results (from author's abstract and research page):
- DRIF earns 'economically large premia' across the 1937-2024 sample
- Results hold across subsamples and alternative research designs
- DRIF remains statistically significant after controlling for the modern factor zoo
- DRIF subsumes most short-horizon anomalies (short-term reversal, MAX effect, idiosyncratic volatility, etc.) and lottery-style anomalies
- DRIF emerges as a key factor in cross-sectional asset pricing tests (F-test significance)

Practical Takeaway:
The DRIF signal is a data-driven aggregation of all information in the past month's daily return distribution. Because elastic-net selects and shrinks coefficients, it avoids overfitting despite having 21 inputs. The signal is estimated out-of-sample (annual re-estimation on historical data) and has 87 years of validation. Implementation requires: track last 21 daily returns per stock, compute DRI score monthly, rank stocks and hold top/bottom decile. Long-only implementation would hold top-decile stocks. Rebalancing: monthly.
